smartlead_campaign: OR #6 | Personalized Line | Forecast Lead Magnet | Without Other Contact Name

thread.subject: Braids and Wigs ad report
thread.body_text:
Maria, saw braids and wigs leans on same day shipping plus local delivery in about an hour, curious how that's been landing.

 separate note: I made Braids and Wigs a 4-min ad fatigue report from your live Meta ads. the one about to die, the duplicate eating budget, which to scale, and a fresh angle to test.

 most ecom brands in your niche usually run the same handful of ads too long. this spots it before ROAS drops.

 worth a look?

 Kyle H.
 Co-founder | OmniRocket

 P.S. if there's a better person for this, point me their way :)

thread.subject: RE: Braids and Wigs ad report
thread.body_text:
Maria - forgot to mention: the report also catches the ad Meta is quietly throttling. it's usually a near-copy of another ad that's eating budget without you noticing.

 Braids and Wigs's is still sitting ready on my end - want me to send it?

 - Kyle

 P.S. if it's not you, who should I reach out to?

thread.subject: RE: Braids and Wigs ad report
thread.body_text:
Maria - last check on this.

 should I shelve Braids and Wigs's fatigue report, or is there a better week to send it? no worries either way - just don't want to keep landing in your inbox.

 - Kyle

 P.S. if timing's just off, ping me in a few weeks - still happy to send.

Hi Kyle,
 

 Thank you for following up. Before we move forward, could you clarify what you'd need from us, specifically, did you need access to our Meta ad account to put this report together and if so, what level?
 

 Best regards,
 

 
 Maria Mushi 
 Manager – Braids and Wigs 
 London, Ontario 
 226-504-1321 
 www.braidsandwigs.ca

Hi Maria,

 great question, and the short answer is nothing needed on your end for what i already built.

here's the report btw: Braids and Wigs Ad Fatigue Report 

 this report was built entirely from public data. i pulled your live ad from meta's ad library and analyzed them from the outside, so no account access was involved at all.

 access only comes into play for the deeper work: a full audit plus a 30 and 90 day plan mapped to your actual numbers.

 the way we work is we hop on a quick call first, i get to understand your brand and where you're trying to go, and if we're a good fit we move forward. that's the point where i'd need access, and we'd start with read-only.

 worth a 30 min chat to see if it's a fit?

 Book a time here → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Braids and Wigs is a value-driven Canadian hair retailer in London Ontario running one weak 'Send message' ad with no reviews on site, so the play is to test three buyer angles and rescue their ad spend, not to promise big scaling numbers.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A Canadian online beauty retailer based in London, Ontario. They sell wigs, braiding hair, extensions, and hair and skin care across many name brands like Outre, Sensationnel, Freetress, and Ampro. They also offer local delivery in London within about an hour.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: About 500 products. Big range in price, from under a dollar for beads to over $500 for premium human hair. The median price is $10.52, so I used that as a stand-in for their average order value. That is the honest read from the public catalog. A lot of their volume looks like it comes from low-cost items like gels, edge control, and accessories, with wigs and bundles as the high-ticket pieces.
deep_dive.positioning: High-variety value play. Big catalog of name brands at low prices with easy pay-later options. They are not a boutique premium house. Their promise is name-brand looks fast, affordably, and with reassurance like a money-back guarantee.
deep_dive.who_buys: Value-conscious Canadian women who wear and style protective hair like wigs, braids, weaves, and crochet. They want the look fast, they care about price, and they like flexible payment.
strengths: Strong offers already in place: free shipping over $69.99, a free gift at $99.99, buy now pay later, and local same day delivery in London.
Wide catalog of recognized brands, so there are many possible angles and price points to test.
gaps: No reviews visible anywhere on the site, so there is no social proof to lean on in ads. This is the single biggest weakness for running paid traffic.
Only one live Meta ad and it uses 'Send message' as the goal, not a purchase. That is a weak setup if the aim is online sales, and it is likely why the Fatigue Forecast flagged them.

game_plan.headline: Find the one winning buyer angle in 30 days by testing three avatars against their real bestsellers, and fix the weak 'Send message' ad setup.

phases.goal: Learn which buyer type and which angle actually drives online sales, and get a clean purchase-focused campaign running instead of the current message ad. This is a learning phase, not a scaling phase.
campaigns: One purchase-focused sales campaign with three ad sets, each built around one of the three avatars below.
One simple retargeting campaign to bring back people who viewed a product or added to cart but did not buy.
lead_products: Human Hair Water Wave Headband Wig ($145.10) and Human Hair Kinky Headband Wig ($123.34): these are their own headline products, glueless and easy install, and the 'zero effort, ready to wear' angle is exactly what a value shopper who wants a fast look responds to. I would lead with the headband wigs because they are the clearest own-brand hero pieces on the site.

Sensationnel Empire Feather Crochet Braids ($58.04) and French Curl Braiding Hair 28 inches ($5.80): lower-priced entry items to pull in braid and protective-style shoppers and test a cheaper price point.
Mane Concept Brown Sugar Wig ($65.29, marked down from $101.57): a strong deal-led piece for the bargain hunter, since the discount is the hook.
phases.creative: Short video or simple before-and-after style clips showing the glueless headband wigs going on fast, plus the water wave and kinky textures moving. Use their own customer words in the copy: 'melted hairline', 'perfect hairline', 'extra hold', 'pre-stretched', 'bring beauty to life without the huge price tag'. Lean hard on the offers as proof points: free shipping over $69.99, buy now pay later, and the free gift at $99.99. Keep three clear angles, one per avatar. Do not make any banned claims about repairing hair, regrowing hair, or healing skin.
phases.budget: Start around $50 to $70 a day total, split across the three ad sets, for the first two weeks. This is a floor to learn from, not a scale number. Adjust in the second two weeks toward whichever angle is getting the cheapest add to cart and sales.
phases.what_success_looks_like: By day 30 we know which of the three avatars buys, which product angle wins, and we have a purchase campaign running that beats the old message ad. We are not promising a revenue jump yet. We are buying certainty about what works.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: Enough to get roughly 30 to 50 clicks a day at typical costs in the beauty and hair category, which is the floor needed to learn which angle and audience work in 30 days.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We would set the target after the call, once we know their real average order value and margin
estimates.basis: Public data only shows a median product price of $10.52, which is a single item, not a full basket. Their offers push people to a $69.99 free-shipping and $99.99 free-gift threshold, so real order value is likely higher, but we cannot see it. Guessing a revenue number now would be dishonest.

assumptions: They want online sales, not just Instagram messages. If their business is mostly local walk-in and DM orders, the whole plan changes.
Their real average order value is higher than the $10.52 median item price, because of free-shipping and free-gift thresholds pushing bigger baskets. We must confirm this on the call.
The headband wigs and marked-down wigs are among their better sellers. This is a guess from the catalog and needs confirming.
There is enough margin on the mid and high ticket wigs to pay for ads. Cheap items like $5 braiding hair and $1 beads cannot carry paid traffic alone.

questions.question: Are you spending on Meta ads right now, and about how much a month?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is any current budget. We only saw one live ad. If they spend almost nothing, the plan is a first real test. If they spend a lot, we need to know where it goes.

questions.question: Who handles your ads today, you, someone in-house, or another agency?
questions.listening_for: Whether we are replacing someone, whether Maria makes the call, or if there is a decision maker not on this call.

questions.question: What is your average order value, and do most orders clear that $69.99 free-shipping mark?
questions.listening_for: This is the key number. The public median item price is only $10.52. We need to know the real basket size before we can promise anything. Listen for whether baskets are big enough to carry ads.

questions.question: On your best-selling items, roughly what is left after product cost and shipping?
questions.listening_for: Margin. If cheap items are the bulk of sales with thin margin, paid ads will struggle. We need room to pay for clicks and still profit.

questions.question: Have you run paid ads before, and what happened?
questions.listening_for: Past wins and burns. If they tried and it flopped, why. This tells us if the problem was creative, offer, or targeting.

questions.question: Your live ad uses 'Send message' as the button. Are you selling mostly through DMs, or do you want people buying on the website?
questions.listening_for: This is the single biggest fork. If they sell by DM, the funnel is different. If they want website sales, the current ad goal is wrong and that is an easy fix to point to.

questions.question: What are your actual top three or four best-selling products?
questions.listening_for: Whether my guesses about the headband wigs and marked-down wigs are right. Their answer directly changes which products we lead the ads with.

questions.question: Do you have any customer photos, videos, or reviews we could use, even from Instagram or DMs?
questions.listening_for: Social proof. The site shows zero reviews. If they have testimonials or install photos sitting in their phone, that fixes our biggest creative gap fast.

questions.question: How much of your business is local London delivery versus shipping across Canada?
questions.listening_for: Whether we target all of Canada or lean into the fast local delivery angle. The one-hour local delivery is a real hook if a chunk of sales are local.

questions.question: When someone lands on a product page, do they usually buy, or do they add to cart and vanish?
questions.listening_for: Where the funnel leaks. If traffic is fine but nobody buys, the problem is the page or the offer, not the ad.

questions.question: Do you get many people finding you on Google by searching product names like 'Sensationnel wig Canada' or 'braiding hair London Ontario'?
questions.listening_for: Whether search demand exists. People clearly search for these brand names and for local hair shops, so there is a real Google and local SEO opening here. Listen for whether they already get any of it.

questions.question: When people search your brand name 'Braids and Wigs', do you show up cleanly, or do competitors show above you?
questions.listening_for: Brand search protection. If competitors bid on their name or outrank them, that is a cheap Google fix worth raising.

objections.objection: We mostly sell through Instagram messages and local delivery, so why change the ad?
objections.answer: That is fine, and we can keep that working. But a message ad only reaches people ready to chat now. A purchase ad pulls in buyers who want to check out on their own, day or night, including all the Canada-wide shipping customers you are not capturing. We would test it against your current setup, not replace it blind.

objections.objection: Our products are cheap, we cannot afford to pay for ads.
objections.answer: Some are, and that is why we would not push the $5 braiding hair by itself. We would lead with your headband wigs and marked-down wigs where there is room to pay for a click and still profit. Your free shipping at $69.99 and free gift at $99.99 already nudge people to bigger baskets, which is exactly what makes ads work.

objections.objection: We do not have reviews or fancy content to run ads with.
objections.answer: That is honestly your biggest gap right now, and it is fixable. Even phone photos of installs, or a few happy DM screenshots, become strong ad material. Part of the first 30 days is getting simple proof in front of buyers. We do not need a studio.

objections.objection: How do I know this will work before I spend?
objections.answer: You do not, and I will not promise a number I cannot see. That is the point of the first 30 days. We spend a small learning budget to find out which buyer and which product actually sells for you. After that we know what to scale, instead of guessing.

red_flags.flag: No reviews anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Paid ads lean heavily on social proof to turn a cold click into a sale. With zero visible reviews, cold traffic has no reason to trust a new brand. This must be fixed early or results will suffer.

If most orders really are one cheap item, there is not enough margin to pay for ads and profit. We must confirm the real basket size on the call before promising anything. The whole plan depends on baskets being bigger than one $10 item.

red_flags.flag: The one live ad uses 'Send message', not a purchase goal.
red_flags.why_it_matters: If they want website sales, this setup is pointed at the wrong outcome and Meta is optimizing for chats, not buyers. It may also mean their real sales channel is DMs, which would change the whole approach.

red_flags.flag: Currency and site setup look messy, with a huge scrambled currency list on every page.
red_flags.why_it_matters: A confusing storefront can hurt conversion. Worth a light check that checkout works cleanly for Canadian buyers before we send paid traffic to it.

other_channel_openings: Google Search: shoppers actively search brand names like Sensationnel, Outre, and Freetress, plus terms like 'braiding hair Canada'. That is real existing demand we could capture cheaply.
Local SEO and Google Business: they offer one-hour local delivery in London, Ontario, so ranking for local hair shop searches is a clear and honest opening.
Brand search protection on Google: making sure they own the top spot when someone searches 'Braids and Wigs' directly.
